planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring before emergence or in fall after dormancy in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil; trilliums are slow to establish and resent disturbance — never purchase wild-collected plants. Yellow trillium goes summer-dormant in June–July in the wild but may remain green longer in cultivation with consistent moisture.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; yellow trillium is a native woodland wildflower of the southern Appalachians — common in rich cove forests of North Carolina, Tennessee, and Georgia — producing the characteristic trio of leaves and a single erect, sessile (stalkless) flower in clear, warm yellow. The lemon-yellow petals and prominent maroon-yellow stamens give the flower a tropical richness distinct from the white and red trilliums. The mottled silver-green leaves are attractively marked year-round. Yellow trillium is notable as a lemon-scented trillium — the flowers have a pleasant citrusy fragrance that distinguishes it from other sessile-flowered trilliums. It is among the showiest of the sessile trilliums and particularly well suited to shade gardens in the Upper South. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants, as collection from the wild is illegal and ecologically harmful.

planting.note: Divide established rhizomes in early spring before shoots emerge; Paris polyphylla requires consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full shade. Seeds require double dormancy (warm, then cold stratification) and take 2+ years to germinate; division is far faster. Plants take years to reach flowering size — patience is essential.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in full to deep shade; Paris polyphylla (Chinese Paris, seven leaves one flower) is a remarkable Asian woodland perennial that looks unlike any other garden plant: a single, stout stem rises to produce a precise whorl of 5–10 lance-shaped leaves around its circumference, then produces from the center a single bizarre spidery flower with narrow, thread-like green petals surrounding a prominent ovary, ultimately developing into a dramatic, fleshy red or black seed capsule that splits open to reveal scarlet seeds. The architectural precision of the whorled leaves and the alien appearance of the flower make it one of the most distinctive plants for the shade garden collector. Native to forest understories from the Himalayas through China and Southeast Asia, it is increasingly available from specialty nurseries. It is one of the most important traditional medicinal plants in Chinese herbalism (Yun Nan Bai Yao).

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in acid, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil with afternoon shade; stewartia transplants best when young and small and establishes slowly. It dislikes root disturbance and should be sited carefully as a permanent specimen. Requires consistent moisture and does not tolerate drought or compaction.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil with morning sun and afternoon shade; Japanese stewartia is widely considered one of the finest four-season landscape trees available, earning distinction in every month of the year: brilliant white, camellia-like flowers with orange stamens bloom in June–July during the quiet gap between spring and late-summer bloomers; large, leathery leaves turn orange-red in fall; and the stunning exfoliating bark reveals a patchwork mosaic of gray, orange-brown, cream, and reddish-brown that makes it arguably the most beautiful bark of any deciduous tree in temperate horticulture. The pyramidal growth habit is elegant and requires little pruning. Trees grow slowly but steadily to 20–30 feet over many decades and are extremely long-lived. Unlike the native Stewartias (S. ovata, S. malacodendron), Japanese stewartia is considerably more cold-hardy and easier to establish in most garden conditions.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in warm, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun after all frost danger has passed; neem is very frost-sensitive and is killed at 32°F. It grows extremely fast in ideal tropical conditions — up to 10 feet per year — and is remarkably drought-tolerant once established. Seeds lose viability within weeks of ripening and should be planted fresh.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to fertile soil in full sun in a frost-free location; neem is one of the most multipurpose trees in tropical horticulture, revered for thousands of years in India where virtually every part of the tree is used: the seeds yield neem oil, widely used as an organic pesticide and insect repellent containing azadirachtin, a potent botanical insecticide; the leaves are used medicinally for their antibacterial, antifungal, and antiparasitic properties; young shoots and leaves are eaten as a bitter vegetable in some South and Southeast Asian cuisines; and the twigs are traditionally used as disposable toothbrushes (chew sticks). The tree itself is a spectacular ornamental — fast-growing with a broad, spreading crown of dense, pinnate leaves and clusters of fragrant white flowers beloved by bees. It is one of the most drought-tolerant trees available and tolerates alkaline, poor soils that support little else. The organic pesticide market for neem oil products has created significant commercial cultivation in Florida and Hawaii.

planting.note: Broadcast fonio seed thinly onto a well-prepared, fine seedbed after last frost; the seeds are tiny (smaller than sesame) and need only shallow soil contact and moisture to germinate. Fonio tolerates very poor, dry, acidic, sandy soils where other grains fail — do not over-fertilize as excessive nitrogen produces excessive vegetative growth at the expense of grain.

directions: Broadcast seed densely onto a fine, firm seedbed after last frost in full sun; fonio (acha) is one of Africa's oldest cultivated grains, grown for at least 5,000 years across the Sahel and West Africa in the semi-arid regions of Senegal, Guinea, Mali, Burkina Faso, and Nigeria, where it is the first grain to be harvested each season and a critical food security crop. The grain is tiny — resembling a grain of sand — but nutritionally remarkable: gluten-free, high in essential amino acids methionine and cystine that are deficient in most other grains, and with a pleasant, slightly nutty, delicate flavor that is different from any familiar grain. Cooked fonio is fluffy and lighter than couscous. It is increasingly available in US natural food stores and is experiencing a culinary renaissance promoted by West African chefs. As a crop, it thrives in hot, dry, marginal conditions and completes its cycle in just 70–90 days, making it exceptionally productive per day of growing season in difficult conditions.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops
